title: 2026 04 22 - HE
description: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission Karen Orsborn, Chief Executive Hayden

chunks.text: Good morning, everybody. It's very good to have you here. How are you? Jim Caron Osborne and Hayden Wainow and Dr Barbara Disley. I'll let you introduce yourselves as well, but I understand we have you here for two hours today. I'll let you open up with some introductory remarks, such as an introduction, and then I'll have the two members to ask questions over to you. Thank you for making the time. Thank you. Thank you for the opportunity to allow us to present our last year's work, and perhaps

a look into what we're looking into in the future. As many of you will know that we were set up following the inquiry into mental health and addiction back in 2018, and we came into form in February 21. So six years of existence at the time, the Commission was set up to be a force for change, helping to lead a shift to a mental health wellbeing system that is accountable and is experienced as joined up, respectful, responsive, by tongue to fire order, their families, and whānau. The system is serving a population now that is poorer in mental health and wellbeing,

an increasing need for mental health support compared to six years ago. There have been improvements. More staff in place due to filling vacancies has been a key driver to improve access rates and reduced wait times. Over 236,000 people have seen access to the Access and Choice Program in 2024, but less than the expected target of 325,000 per annum. However, for young people, access to specialist mental health services reduced, they faced longer wait times, had more referrals, declined. This is particularly concerning given the rate of psychological distress that is increasing for young people. In 2024 and 25, was the coming over age for

Te Hiringa Mahata? Our system performance monitor showed a system new linking outcomes for people with contributions from the Government. Our first system performance monitoring report outlined what a good mental health and addiction system looks like. The report shows that while there are pockets of positive change, there is a significant way to go to achieve the outcomes people expect. Our annual stakeholder engagement survey told us there is an overall positive deception of Te Hiringa Mahata. Stakeholders rated us highly on our engagement, reliability of advice, reports and information that we publish, and speaking up on important

mental health, addiction and wellbeing issues. Māori, iwi and lived experience stakeholders report positive engagement with us. We can see the impact of our work in three key areas, the Access and Choice Program, the work to repeal and replace the Mental Health Act, and the new Mental Health and Wellbeing Strategy. The launch of the Access and Choice Program represented a major investment in mental health and addiction. Our monitoring has provided transparency and accountability. In response to our findings, we saw greater investment in targeted action and improvement in data quality

and visibility. Since our establishment, we have advocated for a mental health and addiction system that places people and whānau at the centre. Central to this has been our support for the work to repeal and replace the Mental Health Act. We support legislative and practice changes that uphold the rights, people's rights, to make informed decisions about their care and treatment. We have advocated for stronger recognition of mental health and wellbeing within legislation. Our evidence-based advice made the case for a dedicated mental health and wellbeing strategy anchored in research and shaped by the voices of people with lived experience. Our engagement has been acknowledged and actively sought with the Minister for

Mental Health supporting our ongoing involvement in the development of the mental health and wellbeing strategy. In the coming year ahead, our 2526 is our first year under our new strategy and statement of intent. We have used strategic priorities focused on mental health and addiction services for tamata whāia ora and outcomes for people with lived experience and on improving the performance of the mental health and addiction system. We're focusing our efforts over the next three years through making three strategic shifts on how we work, placing people with lived experience at the centre of our work, being future-oriented, promoting alignment and

leveraging knowledge to influence positive change, and being a bold advocate. The first Hauora Heningaro Tiara Tu Rua forum was a success with over 240 attendees. Feedback was positive, particularly about the timely announcement regarding crisis response funding. Most delegates surveyed found the program relevant, topical and useful. The publication of Urupare Moreria Crisis Response Monitoring Report was a significant milestone. Analysis of media cover showed 23 articles across major outlets in an estimated reach of 2.8 million impressions. We published the assessment of progress

implementation of kōtimata to Haringa recommendations. This is the first time that we have assessed progress with recommendations that we've made. Of the five recommendations made in early 2024, three have been completed by Health NZ and the government. Two others are then worked on. This year, in 2026, the Minister for Mental Health will undertake a review of our factors that was built into the legislation from the beginning and the operations of the Haringa Mahara. Our case studies highlight the system is shifting. We are a trusted voice in speaking up on important health, a mental health addiction, and well-being issues. We recognise much more needs to be done

and will take time, sustained leadership and action. The new mental health and well-being strategy and our ongoing oversight, monitoring and advocacy will be critical to seeing the change people and whānau want to see. Thank you. Thank you very much for that. Some questions and good lyrics. Yeah, thank you very much and lovely to see you all. I'm interested to pick up some of the themes that you mentioned in your introduction and clear that when you say a trusted voice, that certainly is the perceptions in the sector. I'm curious to know some things from a systems perspective that your commission has highlighted. One of the things we asked you about a number of

years ago was leadership in the mental health sector. I'm wanting to get a sense from you whether you feel that the leadership is there and I'm thinking also about accountability given that we have more agencies who are supposed to or have a statutory duty to provide some accountability. But when we ask them all, fingers go the other way or they just isn't the legislative teeth. And in fact, when I look, I see accountability is only really at a contracted level, which are very output-orientated. So there's two systems levels questions there. One is the leadership there. Secondly, are you satisfied that the accountability are baked into the system in the way they should be around mental health? Let me start. I think we can all probably have a bit of a go at that question. It's a big one.

In terms of leadership, we've seen some change in improvement, particularly within Health New Zealand. And we've been able to actively engage at the governance and operation, senior operational level. So we've seen a deficit, I suppose, of leadership in that space that has been filled in recent times. In terms of accountability, I think there's still ways to go. We think that the mental health and wellbeing strategy is an important one, that it has an overlay that includes within it milestones and accountabilities that you refer to. I can give one example of where system change leadership, we particularly paid a role in

that is in the crisis response space. There was a lack of clear leadership across the various different agencies, police, justice and health. And we managed to fill avoid. And I think as a result of that, there was some progress made and then governments subsequently made some investments. So change is happening in pockets, but I would still say in terms of overall accountability and a joint up system, we have long ways to go. And as I said in that introduction, it's going to require time and sustained leadership for us to get there. That's a starter, but all Karen. Thank you for that. And I guess that leads me into a pivot, if you like, or a focus now on youth. And I know that in your latest report, we're still seeing youth having

longer wait times to access specialist services. And in fact, we're seeing longer wait times for addiction services as well. So I'm interested to know whether the focus on youth is, whether you're able within the current architecture to include some of the really important things like social determinants and other elements that might make a significant difference lifting that access and engagement. I think there's two parts to your question. In terms of this part of your question. Sorry, can you clarify that? So the best part is around the wait times for 19 to 24 year olds. So if we're unpacking, we're unpacking it up, that is probably the best one line statement we can

say. What we're finding for young people is the referrals are being made, but they're being declined. And so understanding what that's about is important to us. Is that work happening? Do you know that the tracking of the wait times? Okay, I can comment on that. So we've had a long-term focus on rangatahi and young people looking at both what are the drivers for the increasing rates of distress in that sort of determinant space and reported on the well-being outcomes rangatahi and youth as well as access to services. So both is really important. So we are, you've seen youth reflected in our crisis and other work, and we are doing a deep dive this year to really try and understand those pathways across the system, but also the access patterns.

So what we have seen is, as Haley said, a continued decline in the numbers of young people accessing specialist services. And there are more options in primary and community, but we're still hearing a lot about the concerns that people have about young people not being able to get access when they need it. So we need to understand how that whole system is working. Oh yeah, but I want to stop on the same line. Thank you. Just final stop, thank you for that. Last night when I asked the minister about this, he said that people are being referred to different organizations or different specialists. That's why there are these declines. Have you seen evidence that young people are actually

being deferred into services and receiving the treatment, or is that a statement that needs some evidence behind it? Yeah, look, we have heard that referrals are being declined because there's more appropriate services. And we know there are more options in those primary and community services, particularly like the the youth stream of access and choice and other options. We don't fully understand the detail that sits behind that, which is what we're hoping to understand more in the deep dive that we're doing this year. So there is something happening in terms of that 18 to 24 age group who are accessing adult services. So wanting to understand what's happening within services and why young people, that age group has continued to decline while adults has

increased. So there is something under there that we need to understand. So we're looking at it. Which is what my sub was going to be about, is that transition from youth to adult and being able to understand what is the gap or the blockage for that transition, if you can have any comment on that? Yeah, look, we haven't particularly looked at that transition. And so that is part of the work we're doing this year as part of the deep dive looking at young people and how that transition works. Because we have heard many young people are all different. And so for some, they may be more ready to transition into adult services. Others might prefer to actually be continued to be supportive in the services they're currently receiving. So that as said, that's what we're looking at this year. Right, so you're going to

create to understand what that blockage or yes, yes. And so in our work, what we do is we have the board brush approach to monitoring. And then we highlight the areas where we need to have a closer look. And last year, we was really clear that crisis responses was an area we needed to have a closer look. So we did that deep dive last year. And this year, it's really having a look at those youth services. And that's how we work as an entity is to say, where do we need to have a closer look? And so that there's a gap in that transition. But at the moment, yeah, that's what we need to understand more. And that's what we add as value to the system is being able to look at those deep dives, bring some international evidence. What is working well in New Zealand? What can we learn across the system? So

that's that's what we're doing. Thank you, Mr Chair. And thank you for being here and for your Mahi. I know that Arangatahi have some of the worst statistics for mental health and the OECD. And I want to talk about your advocacy role, particularly with other ministries and ministers. So we know that the new minister stamp for its new curriculum, particularly the health of physical education curriculum, does not mention the words mental health at all. How have you advocated to the minister or the Ministry for Education to have mental health within the curriculum? We haven't had any specific advocacy

when we have those relationships across government. And certainly we know that other government entities agencies draw from from our work. And so we certainly advocate for youth voice so for young people to have a voice and whatever changes are being proposed. But also for young people to have greater knowledge about mental health and wellbeing, what they can do to keep themselves well and safe. So yeah, we certainly advocate for that. Thank you, Supplementary. And so do you think it is vital for youth and Anata to get support and understanding through their education? At school? Yeah, this is personal response. Yes. Have we done any

particular work in a specific area where we haven't? And supplementary. And so does the Ministry for Education or the Minister Education reached out to you for that advice? I'm not aware of that. Thank you. And one more. If that's okay. It's okay. Thank you. So has there been any discussion or thoughts around having a cross ministry group that meets, like the group that was set up after Te Aurete Kura for the Prevention of Family and Sexual Violence was set up where they came together, talked about mental health, particularly around youth for this case? Has there been any work or thoughts around that? With the focus on education? With the focus on youth mental health?

I'm not that I'm aware of that. Do you think that, yeah? The closest piece of work that we've been involved in there would be in the process response space. And there have been some short term ministry-led initiatives, but not specifically on this co-poper. There has been work across government and in previous terms of government with drawing to either different ministers with different portfolios to focus on, so the child and youth are well-being. Yeah. Thank you. Very much. That's a break. Thank you. During the year you've updated your strategy, what is the main shifts in your priorities and

what are they based on? Good question. I think a number of factors came to play. I'll start ball rolling, but a number of factors came to play for us. One, we found our straps, I suppose, you could say, in a startup 60 years ago. So we were clear about what our purpose was, and we were clear about the resources that we had. The other part, of course, is that we utilised public service to assist us in engaging with stakeholders. So we listened to a lot of people in effect and helped shape our strategy. So knowing what we were about, knowing the resources that we had, what we heard, that brought us into identifying

six areas to shift where from to, and I think, as carers to cover off on those three areas, but they're quite important for us in terms of driving the decisions that we make, or take on an elsewhere side of the organisation. So last year we did undertake what's called a future excellence horizon, which is common across government. So we interviewed a range of stakeholders and also conducted a survey to get feedback from people about if we were the best organisation we could be, what that would look like. And so what we heard was that we needed to place people's experience much more centered in our work. We needed to work to improve a fragmented system.

So we'd heard a lot about the fragmentation and the lack of leadership. We needed to lift our profile. So we heard quite a lot about our visibility and that we needed to look ahead and think about the demographic changes that we're happening. And so that then led into the refocus of our strategy and our 24-25 annual report. Reports against our previous four strategic priorities. So what we're now working to is a refined set of strategic priorities, which is about focusing on people with lived experience, people who are using inter-health and prediction services, so much more focused on services, continuing to have a focus on broader well-being outcomes and determinants. And the new one is really how we

look at the performance of the mental health and addiction system. And as we've mentioned previously, we did release our first system performance report last year. We are about to release our second one this year in June. And that is where the six shifts that we identified. So we said that the system needs to shift in these ways. And that's really informed the mental health and well-being strategy. So happy to take further questions on that. Just a quick step. In multiple of your answers, you've talked about the stakeholders. Who do you see your stakeholders? Who do you see those people or stakeholders? Who are they? Oh, they're really broad. We have multiple stakeholders. So of course, it's people who have lived experience and the whānau families who support them. And that's a really broad

and diverse group. We have obligations to Māori and whānau as part of our legislation and how we engage with Māori and whānau and prioritise equity for Māori. We have some really important sector stakeholders. So working with people who are providing services, commissioning services to achieve the changes that we want to see across government. We also have stakeholders in terms of what we describe as the priority populations. So in our act, there are a list of 12 populations who have higher prevalence of distress. And so it's important that we connect with those groups as well. So Māori for such like young people,

veterans as they're at list. So it's a really broad group and we're a small entity of around 20 staff. So we have to really think smartly about how we connect in with organisations that represent those populations and with the providers. So both Health New Zealand and NGOs and other providers. So it's a big challenge for us how we keep that commission. I appreciate you've got your priority populations. And I think last year when you're here, I did ask about middle-aged males. They're the group that most are most likely to die by suicide. What are you doing to reach out to stakeholders in that group? Because I suppose one of my fears is that some of these people aren't accessing

mental health support. And so probably very hard for you to pick up because they're not they're not engaging with services. Yeah and I think that's the relationships we have with other agencies and organisations who are connected with those groups because it's hard for us to have the direct reach into those communities given the nature of our organisation and our size. So we do have you know that intentional engagement with the groups that represent. So the rural sector or if it's Pacific peoples. So how do we have those connections and then also more broadly like the mental health foundation and those organisations.

Hayden during your initial briefing you mentioned that case studies show that the system is shifting. Is that in relation to these priorities or were you meaning something different? I was referring to the areas that we've seen in the legislation. Taking up of the strategy for mental health and well-being. Focus on youth and we're also talking about access and choice. So those are the areas that we particularly where we've worked on where we can see some contribution to where those areas of work have shifted. Just to clarify the system shift you're talking about are mainly inputs in terms of

ledges sort of change and systems change rather than outcomes which would take some time to measure. Of course. Thank you. Thank you chair. I read your statement of intent that was published in last year July but I couldn't find anything that talked about the needs of the FX community. In the past five years the risk of mental health issues like depression have been significantly increased in Asian communities. Do you think this is a serious concern that lead more attention? Yes we do. We're not reflecting necessarily all of that work that's been done

but there's some work that's been done through Karen and the team and that will in time surface but that's Karen's in a better position to articulate what we're doing. I mean if you think it's a very serious concern why not a single wording or single strategies have been addressing the needs of our Asian community? It probably reflects the fact that we weren't there at that particular time but it's not to say you know it's it's basically saying we're not we can't get to anything at once so but having said that we're now in a position and we are starting to move into making connections in that particular area. So you're trying to tell me that you don't think there's a needs for Asian community before? We knew that. You knew but

you didn't react? Well again with the small organization like trying to do the best that we can in terms of reaching if you look at all 12 population groups that quite extensive and require quite a lot of effort and resource to engage in in a appropriate way and follow through. So at that particular time it wasn't we clearly weren't in a position to articulate anything. We are in a better position to do that now. All right so what do you think are the main reason affecting the mental health in our afflux community? Like how does the mental health and wellbeing commission support a speaker for them? So I've had a lot of engagement particularly with Asian family services and so we have supported the summits that Kelly and her team have hosted

both in Auckland and then the one in in Parliament so and we do look at how we reflect in Asian communities Asian population in our substantive reports. So for example in our access and choice report we highlighted the needs for Asian people to have access to those access and choice services and in our other reports we also highlight one of the needs for Asian people alongside Maori and Pacific. So we certainly have done work to connect with both Asian family services and other key leaders from the Asian community and to reflect that in our work. Have you removed the barrier for the Asian community to access to mental health services?

We removed the barrier. Any barrier or you just don't you just believe that there's no barrier for for any afflux groups or Asian communities to access to the mental health service? Well our role is to highlight what's happening in terms of access and need and then to advocate for that and in terms of for Asian the Asian population we have been reflecting those needs and doing that advocacy in our work and I think this does highlight the challenges I've been said you know there are 12 priority populations and it's hard to reflect each of those populations specifically particularly in a statement of intent which is around us as an entity but in our substantive work we certainly do everything to bring forward those voices in

our work. If you think if you just mentioned that your main function is to advocate you know advocate for people or give advices what advice have you given to the minister so far to address the needs of our Asian communities and also are you happy with the result you have been implemented in the last 12 months so far if not which areas do you think it needs to improve? We have raised the issue with the minister we haven't got a clear pathway forward but we have raised it as an important area that needs attention and specifically in relation to the Access and Choice Programme and their report at the end of five years and we did highlight the need for a service stream that was specific for our Asian population and the minister responded to

that in a positive way. Just a quick comment and then I'm going to ingrid for a suck and Cam also has a suck. Just let you know I'm afraid this up. How long has it been? And then Kahu has her primary. Oh sorry was a suck. I'm actually not a primary thank you. Quick comment. Just let you know I'm afraid this is a point of the answer you put by today and I expect a better outcome in the next 12 months. Thank you. Thank you for that speaking to Dr Kelly Fing it seems that one of the problems is that you don't know what you don't know when it comes to the Asian mental health issues and gambling in particular so first is that a piece of work that is when you talk about the work that you're doing Karen is that what you're looking at because that baseline

has to be missing and that's probably a big part of why they don't get funding that I think Dr Chung is alluding to is not proportionate to the problem we can't count it. The second part is with access and choice are you aware that the access and choice solution provided by the government is is not the same as the other strands of access and choice is convoluted and therefore makes it really problematic for Asian communities to actually access access and choice and if so what would you like to see done about that? Yeah so just on that the second part I'm aware that the the solution for that that Asian stream access and choice is different from the other streams and that reflects that

those other streams we're right from the beginning and this is a a solution to improve access and I know that's um I haven't talked with Kelly recently but you know that some aspects of that are quite challenging and those do need to be worked through I think ultimately it is about people being able to access the services supports in a timely way so it is about the specific streams and it is also about the appropriateness of all services for people who are accessing those services um the um you know the Asian population is is made up of different types of population as it is for Pacific peoples as well so it is how do we yeah as said have the specific streams and services that meet everybody's needs in the most appropriate way and so is the

baselining of the how big is the problem which is a question and is difficult if the data is only being collected from small data sets of who is accessing and what we're seeing on the ground is a much bigger problem is that a gap in the system and is that something that you are working at all? Yeah that goes to the prevalence study so we have advocated for a proper sort of study of the level of need in communities and we're speaking particularly about the Asian community. Yes well that would that would include what's the level of need for um for Asian communities as well as other communities so the the youth stream is underway and that should give us a really good insights into what is the level of need for um for the all of those populations and then how can services be targeted to meet those needs and particularly for the youth does pick up um that's

part of those changing demographics so um you see that particularly in the at that age group so that will give us some really useful information um but not only us for the whole system so we have a role to play um but we're not the commissioners and funders of services so we're bringing that oversight so we have views and insights and we can feed those in um I think you I think you can say that we are increasing our presence in this area and you will have some uniformness thank you. Cam. Oh thank you chair. Something can be true. Chair, there's a supplementary for your primary. The questions that we've had in this light of question in this light of question we have focused on groups of people um specifically you know heard some some um strong advocacy and questions from um Dr Chulman Asian um Dr Campbell on middle

aged men do you think that labelling listening categorizing the way it's done in this act when you're enabling piece of legislation is actually um making your job harder when a population is divided up and everyone starts fighting who do you think that it's actually that the amount of trust in what you're able to do is being impacted because of the dividing people up and clear the breach of a social contract where we all come together to support each other. Order you think politicians are dividing us. Yeah look I think is it making your job easier or harder? I think it's helpful speaking to the to be to be you know to be I think if we're looking at it from a general how we apply

up the resources that we have um that gives us recognizes that not one shoe uh or one solution fits all so but it does provide a challenge for us to when it comes to applying that in practically there are some challenges for us to be able to move effectively across all of those bases. Is that because there's contradictions in the act between things like I think it's section 10 where you can as a commission can decide populations you want to focus on but then also you've got clause 13 schedule two with these lists of populations you have to justify um actually looking at wider population basis like is that is this making I'm guess I'm trying to figure out like you've heard some emotion I would say there's a lot of people who would read this legislation

and yeah you can say it's politician and say are you actually able to look at the population and identify based on what you find what do you feel directed by to these population bases. I had a comment because it is one of the shifts that we've made in our strategy so when we started you know in the early period of time we did do a report focused on the well-being outcomes for Pacific peoples to focus on a particular population group now that report was incredibly well received by the Pacific community um but when we've looked at our resources and as you know we're you know five million dollars and and 20 staff we and the we what we went through with the future excellence horizon the advice was that we needed to be much more focused on people

with lived experience and people who are accessing services and so we have made a shift to um and it was a challenging shift for us to make because we would like to be able to do a report like that on each of the priority populations but we simply don't have the resources to enable us to do that so we have focused on the people who are um accessing services who are using services and the people with distress and trying to bring the voices of those different priority populations into that work so it is hard to disaggregate because it's not that um you know you can have people have more than one of those or can be categorized into more more than one of those categories so it's not just a straight distinction um so it is a challenge for us that we've had

to grapple with thank you mr chair um well we know too many asian people and arty or don't feel welcome and have um mental health and my my colleague from the national party dr callous chung um talked about the need for culturally appropriate services and getting support populations um who are disproportionately affected for example people living in rural areas like farmers um when we're talking about asian family services uh it could look from the outside like they get quite a lot of funding um through other um services um but we know that those that funding is ring

fenced for gambling um and that that means that other um mental health services um which maybe are more needed um in different areas are not able to be funded because it is being fenced for gambling and so my question is around that access and um peer support and what are your suggestions for more flexible contracts um and reactive um contracts for for the need um what's your advice to funders of um mental health services yeah and look i that generally we see a lot of our advice um that

we provide is his own debt policy makers and decision makers and we think the levers of commissioning are really important leaders for change so we don't have our hand directly on those levers but what we can do is shine the light by bringing information to to uh to bear so that hopefully that influences an impossible way and i think it's important that we're not seeing as the fixes of issues because that's just not what we're made no we're designed to do so uh commissioning is a really important area of for change as i've said and i think the kinds of things that you're looking for usually particularly if you want community-based responses is high trust uh flexibility and and ten year term clear on being clear on the expectation

and clear on the outcome that you're looking for really clear steps and measures that are put in place uh and um allowing that community to determine the best way so you're not using that type lose type principle in clear on what you're looking for being clear and what you're measuring and allowing that community to shape but what the response needs to be i'll just make another comment there but in terms of the way we are looking at data or the systems looking at data um in terms of being clearer about the impact across different ethnicities age groups are the factors that might um play into this space it is that that's really important to allow us to be able to look down into those various um variables about what are the presenting

needs what's the difference being made what are the things that would make a difference for particular populations or age groups and what might we be doing about it in commissioning space and i guess what we're seeing is um a higher level of focus on some of those different categories which may not have been there in the past but does allow greater information and so as access and choice and those community programs roll out we're in a better position now to see who's getting access and who isn't and what might need to be done about that and i think that's the area we'll be in in the next few years thank you mr chair i'd like to ask some questions which

i intended uh to get from you a model for me to better understand your um your and other mental health data in the future i notice that your um letter of expectation includes analysis of current resourcing against population needs to inform future areas of focus at a system level often when we talk about health service planning we have a conceptual model of how we might measure need in the community and therefore the service response when we read reports like the one you recently published 202.9 percent of people age 15 to 24 experience psychological high or very high levels

of psychological distress can you please explain the conceptual model that sits behind that measurement or tell me another measurement but the measurement of need and what the services could or should be how do you link that so what we've used in terms of that 22.9 percent is using the health survey results so that as you know has limitations so what what we so that's being used as a proxy to say what what do we um what's the best information we have about the level of need but it's the

prevalence study will give us far better information about the actual level of need and prevalence i don't want to get a quick into a discussion about on Mr Deputy Chair um if i may uh i don't want to get a discussion about measurement and methods um what i'm asking is whatever good measurement you use yeah how will you match that or link it to service need does every person who reports experiencing distress need a clinical service um or not and can you explain that link to me so what i would say is our work is um in terms of uh that system performance view is is and i'll

refer back to the system performance work as we had our first report last year which is where we're and it might not be the one that you're referring to but we're trying to say what are the population level outcomes for for all people and you know we're the well-being for all people in RTI and how do you then draw connections back into the system and into the service contribution and and there's not that direct connection through but what we're trying in terms of attribution you know as many other factors that sit outside of the mental health system um financial crisis um you know wars all of those things actually influences influence people's mental health and well-being so we're trying to draw that that um connection but

the report from last year was year one um and that work will develop over time but it's trying to develop those insights and knowledge about um where we need to do more work and to start to paint the picture so i can um i think i understand we're trying to go with that but it's we've got a long way to get to before we can really say what is the level of need and what is the appropriate response be it in a all-of-government type approach in terms of improving education employment or is it about um what are the services because it's not everybody needs a service in that way it could be more um preventative action some of the well-being support that can be provided so that's the complexity of what we're trying to understand
